articles.title: Wake Up, Remix! Everything's Changing..
articles.description: Big news from the Remix camp this week. About a year ago, Remix and React Router merged together reflecting their shared goals and code, but now it’s all change again. React Router is now basically what Remix originally intended to be, and so ‘Remix’ is rebooting as a model-first, low-dependency, Web API-centric full-stack framework built on Preact. It’ll no longer be a 'React framework' per se.
articles.author: Michael Jackson and Ryan Florence

articles.title: 🕒 The Upcoming Temporal API and What Problems It Will Solve
articles.description: The Temporal API has been cooking for many years now as a new way to work with dates and times in JavaScript. It’s just been enabled in Firefox 139 by default and sure to come to more runtimes ASAP. Here’s why it matters and what it offers.
articles.author: Suren Enfiajyan

articles.title: Announcing Angular v20
articles.description: A big release for the Google backed framework where a lot of the more recent experimental features (like signals and incremental hydration) have received a lot of polish and been promoted as stable. There are new experimental APIs too, including resource streaming and httpResource for making HTTP requests with a signal-based reactive API.
articles.author: Minko Gechev

articles.title: Why Are 2025/05/28 and 2025-05-28 Different Days in JavaScript?
articles.description: An interesting ‘Wat’-like moment in just the first few lines of this post, which Brandon decided to dig into and explain.
articles.author: Brandon Dong

articles.title: ▶  Modern Performance Debugging in Chrome DevTools
articles.description: Paul shares a look at the reimagined Chrome DevTools Performance panel, showing off new features, how they work, and how to use them to make performance gains.
articles.author: Paul Irish

articles.title: React, Visualized: A Visual Exploration of React Concepts
articles.description: The creators of a React course have updated their popular visual explainer of numerous core React concepts to cover React 19 and features like actions, transitions, and Server Components too.
articles.author: Tyler McGinnis et al.

articles.title: ▶  The 3 Ways JavaScript Frameworks Render the DOM
articles.description: The creator of the SolidJS framework looks at the different approaches frameworks take to render what they produce. A good look under the hood that isn’t too complex. (16 minutes.)
articles.author: Ryan Carniato

articles.title: Build a Blog with Angular in Under 30 Minutes Using Analog
articles.description: Analog is a meta-framework for Angular (think along the lines of Next.js or Nuxt).
articles.author: Peter Mbanugo

articles.title: Your React Meta-Framework Feels Broken, Here's Why
articles.description: “Sometimes you’ve got to burn it all down, start from ash, and rebuild from first principles. This is what we did with RedwoodSDK.”
articles.author: Redwood Team

articles.title: Svelte Flow 1.0: Create Node-Based UIs and Diagrams in Svelte
articles.description: From the creators of the similar React Flow comes a customizable Svelte component for building node-based editors and interactive diagrams. Want examples?
articles.author: webkid GmbH

articles.title: Cap: A Lightweight, Modern Open Source CAPTCHA Alternative
articles.description: This solution boasts a method called “proof-of-work” which attempts to make automated CAPTCHA solving more expensive. It uses a Web Component and you can try out a few demos here. GitHub repo.
articles.author: Cap

articles.title: Google Gen AI SDK for TypeScript and JavaScript v1
articles.description: Why let Python developers have all the fun? Now you can harness the full power of Google’s Gemini API (and Vertex platform) from Node.js too.
articles.author: Google

articles.title: ReactJust: Server Components Without a Framework
articles.description: If, for some reason, you don’t want to adopt a full-scale framework like Next.js or React Router to use or experiment with RSCs, this ‘vanilla RSC’ approach could be of interest.
articles.author: almadoro

articles.description: Matteo Collina has unveiled php-node, a way to run PHP apps within the same process as Node.js. Run WordPress with a Next.js frontend? Sure.
TC39's 108th meeting is currently taking place with many proposals being advanced, such as Seeded Pseudo-Random Numbers moving to stage 2 and Error.isError moving to stage 4.
The OpenJS Foundation is now a CVE Numbering Authority (CNA) for over 40 popular JavaScript projects it hosts, including ESLint, Express, and Electron.
Node's technical steering committee has decided against instituting an official bounty program.
Bun v1.2.15 – Gains a bun audit tool for performing security audits of project dependencies.
Ink 6.0 – Use React to build CLI apps. Now supporting React 19.
Docusaurus v3.8 – Popular docs-oriented static site generator.
Ember 6.4, JSPM 4.0

articles.title: Exploring the OKLCH Ecosystem and Its Tools
articles.description: A solid overview of why you may want to start using OKLCH color (for which browser support is now very good), what the essential tools you need to know about are, and what best practices you need to be aware of. There’s an interesting related talk titled ‘▶️ Programmable Colors: Bridging Design and Code’ that’s worth a watch too.
articles.author: Nazarova, Objartel, Turner (Evil Martians)

articles.title: WebStatus.dev: Now with More Data, Deeper Insights, and a Clearer Path to Baseline
articles.description: The open-source Web Platform Status site allows us to query and track various features and what browsers they play nice with. It’s had a notable update recently, with expanded feature coverage, usage timelines, mobile data, and more. Handy!
articles.author: Kadir Topal

articles.title: ▶  CSS if() Functions & reading-flow (in Chrome 137)
articles.description: A solid and straightforward demo of what we can do with the if function (in just Chrome and Edge for now). Powerful stuff. The new reading-flow property is also quickly explained here.
articles.author: Una Kravets

articles.title: Getting Creative with HTML Dialog
articles.description: As you can see above, Andy shares how to spruce up generic looking dialogue boxes with CSS — via animations, the backdrop-filter property, and more.
articles.author: Andy Clarke

articles.title: IE6, AI, and The Future of Browsing the Web
articles.description: A new series on how AI is changing the web. This first entry posits that “Chrome is the next IE6”, and explores how Google’s supposed “retreat from web standards signals a new era of browser stagnation”.
articles.author: RL Nabors

articles.title: ▶  The Three Ways JavaScript Frameworks Render the DOM
articles.description: The creator of the SolidJS framework looks at the different approaches frameworks take to render what they produce. A good look under the hood that isn’t too complex. (16 minutes.)
articles.author: Ryan Carniato

articles.title: CSS Spotlight Effect
articles.description: Because we can feed the current mouse position to CSS with a few lines of JavaScript we can build some neat interaction patterns. Amit highlights (pun intended) one such effect here “that’s easy to create, simple to customize, and looks surprisingly slick”.
articles.author: Amit Sheen

articles.title: Better CSS Shapes Using shape(): More on Arcs
articles.description: Temani continues spelunking into all things CSS shapes, this time exploring the arc command in detail.
articles.author: Temani Afif

articles.title: Beachpatrol: A CLI Tool to Automate Your Everyday Web Browser
articles.description: A higher level way to use Playwright on macOS or Linux to control a regular non-headless browser instance. It’s essentially an approach where you still want a visible browser you can use in a normal way but with added automation possibilities.
articles.author: Sebastian Carlos

articles.title: Smooothy: A Smooth, Extensible Slider/Carousel Component
articles.description: Try out some demos here, which include customizations for keyboard control, parallax, speed, and there are implementations for React and Vue.
articles.author: Federico Valla

articles.title: Glow Icons: 440+ Open Source SVG Icons Made for Interfaces
articles.description: Searchable and available in outline and solid formats. You can also download the icons as Figma files.
articles.author: Alexey Likhobabin

articles.title: MiNi PhotoEditor: An Online WebGL Photo Editor with Effects, Filters, and Cropping
articles.description: Includes some neat features like the before/after slider, lots of filter effects, and the ability to save/load some of the effects as ‘recipes’. Repo here.
articles.author: MiNi

articles.title: Bits UI: Headless Svelte Components to Build Your Own Component Library
articles.description: Includes 40+ open source, unstyled, and accessible components along with utilities, helpers, and lots of good documentation for implementation.
articles.author: Bits UI

articles.title: Font Generator: Generate Fancy Text with Unicode Characters
articles.description: Type some text and this tool will generate endless fancy font options in plain Unicode that you can copy and paste. Examples: 𝔽𝕣𝕠𝕟𝕥𝕖𝕟𝕕 𝔽𝕠𝕔𝕦𝕤, snɔoℲ puǝʇuoɹℲ, F͜͡r͜͡o͜͡n͜͡t͜͡e͜͡n͜͡d͜͡ F͜͡o͜͡c͜͡u͜͡s͜͡, etc. However, it's worth exercising caution over accessibility and how certain devices (like screen readers) will handle this.
articles.author: Font Generator
